planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, sandy or gravelly, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; prairie winecup develops a deep, fleshy taproot that makes transplanting difficult after the first season, so direct seeding or planting very small container plants is preferable. Cold stratification of seeds for 30 days improves germination rates. Once established, it is extremely drought-tolerant and should not be irrigated heavily.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; prairie winecup is an outstanding trailing prairie perennial of central North America producing brilliant, deep magenta-wine-colored, chalice-shaped flowers with a white center — among the most vivid and long-lasting flower colors of any low-growing prairie plant. The deeply cut, trailing stems form a spreading mound that spills over rocks and slopes, and the flowers appear from May through October in an almost continuous succession, making it one of the longest-blooming prairie perennials. It is exceptional for dry, sunny slopes and banks where the trailing stems anchor in the soil, for rock gardens, the front of a sunny border, and for spilling over retaining walls. Native to sandy prairies and plains from Montana to Texas, it is fully cold-hardy to zone 3 while simultaneously thriving in the hot, dry summers of the southern Great Plains and Gulf Coast region. The deep taproot stores water and nutrients, making established plants essentially self-sufficient in rainfall once established without supplemental watering.

planting.note: Sow seeds in well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; giant echium is monocarpic, forming a large leafy rosette for 2-3 years before sending up a single massive flower spike and then dying. It self-sows prolifically and forms persistent colonies. It is damaged below 25 degrees F and is best suited to zones 9-11 or sheltered zones 8b locations. Keep protected from frost until the rosette is large and established.

directions: Sow seeds or plant container-grown rosettes in well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; giant echium is one of the most spectacular biennial plants in existence, native to La Palma in the Canary Islands and producing, in its flowering year, a single enormous vertical tower of flowers reaching 8-15 feet in height — a solid column of densely packed, small, blue-violet flowers numbering in the tens of thousands on a single plant. The effect is unlike anything else in horticulture: a rigid, architectural spire of blue that attracts extraordinary concentrations of bumblebees, honeybees, and other pollinators for weeks. After flowering, the plant dies but sets abundant seed, maintaining self-sowing colonies in appropriate conditions. The bold, silver-hairy, strap-like rosette leaves in the juvenile stage are also attractive and architecturally impressive. In zones 9-11, giant echium has become an important ornamental plant in coastal California, parts of the Gulf Coast, and similarly mild climates. Closely related to E. wildpretii (Tower of Jewels, with red-pink flowers) and E. candicans (Pride of Madeira, branching shrub).

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately wet soil in part shade; Caucasian comfrey spreads vigorously by rhizomes and should be given adequate space or planted where spreading is welcome, such as under trees or in a naturalized shade garden. It can be used as a dynamic accumulator like other comfreys, but is grown primarily for its ornamental sky-blue flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Caucasian comfrey is the most ornamental of all the comfrey species, producing intensely azure-blue to sky-blue, nodding, tubular flowers in arching clusters — distinctly different from the murky purple-pink of common comfrey (Symphytum officinale) and the cream flowers of creeping comfrey (S. grandiflorum). The brilliant, clear blue of the flowers from April to June is exceptional in the partly shaded garden and approaches the blue of gentian and pulmonaria. Like all comfreys, it spreads vigorously by rhizomes to form a substantial colony, which makes it excellent as a large-scale groundcover under trees and shrubs, but means it should be planted where it has room to expand. The large, rough, hairy leaves are typical of the genus and effectively suppress weeds once established. Native to moist, fertile slopes and streambanks of the Caucasus mountains, it is cold-hardy to zone 4 and tolerates woodland conditions well.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring every 2-3 years or plant container-grown plants in moist to moderately dry, fertile, neutral soil in full sun; orange sneezeweed blooms June-July, considerably earlier than the common sneezeweed (H. autumnale), which blooms August-September, making it valuable for filling the midsummer bloom gap in the perennial border. All parts of the plant can cause dermatitis in sensitive individuals.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, fertile, neutral soil in full sun; orange sneezeweed is a western North American native of the Rocky Mountains and Sierra Nevada that blooms in June and July — significantly earlier than the common eastern sneezeweed (Helenium autumnale) that flowers in August and September. The large, golden-orange to orange-yellow daisy flowers have drooping, three-lobed ray petals surrounding a prominent, dark-orange to brown-orange central cone, creating a warm, bright, sunflower-like effect. It grows taller and more robust than many heleniums and is an excellent cut flower. Native to mountain meadows, streambanks, and moist subalpine slopes from Oregon to New Mexico, it is fully cold-hardy to zone 3 and adapts well to the humid east as long as soils are well-drained and not excessively wet in winter. Like all heleniums, the flowers are extremely attractive to bees, butterflies, and specialist native bees throughout the bloom period. Divide every 2-3 years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and plant after all frost danger has passed in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; citronella grass is damaged at 32 degrees F and killed below 28 degrees F. In zone 9-10 it grows as a perennial; in cooler zones it can be grown as an annual or overwintered in containers indoors. It is distinct from lemon grass (Cymbopogon citratus) in its scent profile — citronella oil contains citronellol and geraniol rather than the citral-dominant scent of lemon grass.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun in frost-free or frost-protected conditions; citronella grass is a large, clumping, tropical grass native to Sri Lanka that is the primary commercial source of Ceylon citronella oil — the essential oil used in mosquito-repellent candles, torches, sprays, and lotions worldwide. The essential oil from the leaves and stems contains citronellol, geraniol, and related terpenes that mosquitoes find repellent. The plant itself, when its leaves are crushed or bruised, releases a strong, complex citrus-rose fragrance. Growing it in the garden is an attractive proposition — the blue-green, arching grass clumps are handsome and the repellent effect is modest when the plant is growing undisturbed, but crushing a few leaves releases the fragrance. It is completely distinct from lemon grass (C. citratus), which is grown for cooking rather than insect repellency and has a different chemical profile dominated by citral rather than citronellol. In zones 10-12 it is a reliable perennial ornamental grass; in zones 9 it often overwinters with protection.
